2kv9s.authors: Fiona Duffy, Saffron Baldoza, Julian Baudinet, Ian Birch, Emma Broadhurst, Gina Dimitropoulos, Clare Ellison, Karri Gillespie-Smith, Rachel Loomes, Umairah Malik
2kv9s.description: Autistic children and young people receiving eating disorder focused family therapy (FT-ED) for anorexia nervosa (AN) are more likely than their non-Autistic peers to require longer and more intensive treatment. Qualitative evidence suggests that Autistic young people and their parents struggle with a perceived inflexible delivery of FT-ED that can be mis-attuned to autistic needs. Clinicians delivering this care report a lack of confidence when adapting FT-ED to this population, and there is limited guidance on how to do this. This paper describes evidence informed and coproduced guidelines, developed via a series of workshops with Autistic people with lived experience of an eating disorder, parents and carers, clinicians, researchers, and third sector representatives. The intention of these guidelines is to support clinicians in the adaptation of FT-ED for Autistic young people with AN and their families, with the aim of delivering more appropriate and effective care. Young people and their parents or carers may also find these guidelines helpful when advocating for autism-affirming care within FT-ED.

uyhks.authors: Tara Rezapour, Jamie Berry, C. Munro Cullum, Behzad Elahi, Islam Elbanna, Medhat Elsabbahy, Mehdi Farokhnia, Nabila Haque, Jing Jin, Mario Juruena
uyhks.description: Over the past two decades, Neuroscience-Informed Psychoeducation (NIPE) has gradually evolved and become integrated into various therapy modules for psychiatric and neurological disorders. Although NIPE shares certain features with conventional psychoeducation, it is distinguished by key content and structure-related elements. Its content centers on brain-based explanations of the mechanisms and symptoms underlying psychiatric and neurological disorders, as well as the neural processes associated with treatment response, remission, and recovery. NIPE promotes self-awareness and insight and may contributes to motivation for treatment and treatment adherence. It also informs patients, caregivers, and communities how to play an active role in the prevention, treatment, and recovery process based on this mechanistic understanding. Structurally, NIPE is designed to align with how the brain learns and adapts, improving the learning experience to promote more enduring outcomes. By clarifying the processes and goals of brain recovery, increasing self-awareness and self-efficacy, and supporting adherence to prescribed treatments, NIPE may enhance motivation and retention, and facilitate recovery while reducing shame and stigma. This paper provides an introductory overview of NIPE including its definitions and terminology, the current evidence base, clinical implications, and directions for future development.

7bpw4.authors: Nhu Huynh, Christopher Kendall, Evelyn Ayaji, Nicole Roslin, Roxy O'Rourke, Christie Burton, Jacob Vorstman, Jennifer Crosbie
7bpw4.description: Irritability is a transdiagnostic trait associated with a wide range of psychiatric disorders, but the extent to which this co-occurrence is related to genetic factors is unclear. This systematic review aims to provide an overview of studies examining the genetic associations between irritability-related traits and diverse mental health disorders and traits. Studies published up to May 2025 were retrieved from MEDLINE, EMBASE, PsycINFO, Web of Science, and the reference lists of included studies. Studies of human participants reporting quantitative genetic associations using twin or genome-wide genetic analyses (i.e., genome-wide association study [GWAS] and polygenic risk score [PRS] studies) were screened for inclusion based on pre-determined criteria. A total of 30 studies met the inclusion criteria, including six twin and 24 genome-wide genetic analyses. The results indicated shared genetic liability (e.g., positive genetic correlation) between irritability-related traits and specific mental health outcomes, with the most replicated evidence observed for internalizing (i.e., depression; rg=0.46-0.56) and externalizing (i.e., ADHD; rg=0.29-1.00) domains. Emerging evidence for other domains, such as neurodevelopmental disorders, psychotic disorders, and substance use disorders, remained inconclusive due to the limited number of studies on these disorders. Research using standardized measurement of the irritability phenotype and more ancestrally-diverse samples is needed to better understand the genetic architecture of irritability and its transdiagnostic role in mental health disorders.

jbvt6.authors: Hilmar Zech, Shreyash Garg, Roshan Prakash Rane, Markus Reichert, Maria Waltmann, Dominic Reichert, Fabian Arntz, Johannes Steffen, Jens Strehle, Bernd Lenz
jbvt6.description:
Background. Stress is a trigger for alcohol consumption, with working memory capacity (WMC) proposed as a protective factor. While several laboratory-based and cross- sectional studies point toward an interaction between WMC, stress, and alcohol consumption, ecological momentary assessments (EMAs) are needed to establish this relationship in real-life contexts, where alcohol consumption and stress experiences actually take place.
Methods. In this one-year longitudinal EMA study, we collected data from a gamified, smartphone-based WMC task (every 4 weeks) and EMAs of stress and alcohol consumption (every two days) from n=607 participants diagnosed with AUD. We then integrated this data with structural brain imaging data to examine the neural correlates of the hypothesized protective effect of WMC.
Results. As preregistered, we found that WMC moderated the within-subject association between stress and alcohol consumption. Participants with low WMC but not participants with high WMC consumed more alcohol in more stressful compared to less stressful months. In further analysis, within-subject fluctuations in WMC also moderated the within-subject association between stress and drinking. Albeit to the expected direction, a moderating effect of dlPFC volume on the within-subject association between stress and drinking was not significant.
Conclusions. We provide first real-life evidence that greater WMC might increase resilience to stress in individuals with AUD and help them to better control alcohol consumption. This advances our neurocognitive understanding of individual susceptibility to stress-induced drinking, which could inform the development of future, personalized interventions.

g5whd.authors: Tim Palmer, Noémie Goeuriot, Leonie Koban, Elsa Fouragnan, Jacqueline Scholl
g5whd.description: Emotion introspection has been proposed as a key mechanism through which mental health or well-being interventions (such as meditation or psychotherapy) act. However, so far, evidence has almost exclusively come from questionnaire measures. Here, we propose a brain-based measure of emotion introspection. We recruited 25 participants to perform an emotion introspection task after completing a 2-week block of meditation either high or low in body-focus in a cross-over counterbalanced design. Participants viewed and rated emotion pictures that varied from neutral to those evoking negative affect. We obtained trial-wise brain-based measures of negative affect by applying the previously established Picture Induced Negative Emotion brain signature. As a measure of emotion introspection ability, we correlated this brain-based negative affect measure with the trial-wise self-report ratings. We found strong (r=0.58) correlations between brain-based and rated affect with good test-retest reliability (r=0.64) consistent across both sessions regardless of whether it was the 1st or 2nd intervention block. However, this measure was not differentially affected by the type of meditation intervention (i.e. high body-focus ‘body scan’ versus low body-focus ‘self-enquiry’). In a very exploratory analysis, we found some evidence that the brain-based measure of emotion introspection correlated with self-reported introspection ability. In conclusion, we propose a novel approach to robustly assess emotion introspection, going beyond the typical assessments based on questionnaires, while also finding that emotion introspection is not easily changed through targeted training.

qcj45.authors: Shanshan Ma, Erin L Maresh, Andrea M Coppola, Katherine E. Richard, Leonie Koban, Diego Guevara Beltran, David Sbarra, Jessica R. Andrews-Hanna
qcj45.description: Empathy lies at the core of social interactions, particularly in intimate relationships, yet few laboratory tasks are designed to capture empathy in interpersonal contexts—especially those requiring individuals to simultaneously process social information directed toward both the self and a romantic partner. In this exploratory study, 131 adults (from 71 romantic couples) completed a neuroimaging paradigm that manipulated whether positive or negative partner-directed feedback was presented alone or alongside self-directed feedback. The partner-alone feedback contrasts (partner-directed feedback alone versus a neutral control condition) robustly engaged brain regions associated with empathic responding, including the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex, anterior insula, dorsomedial prefrontal cortex and temporoparietal junction. Notably, during the incongruent feedback contrasts—simultaneous conflicting self- and partner-directed feedback versus self-directed feedback alone—participants exhibited diminished affective and brain responses but evidenced unique brain activity in frontoparietal areas frequently associated with cognitive control (e.g., dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and supramarginal gyrus). Furthermore, when responding to positive partner-directed feedback in the presence of negative self-directed feedback, enhanced activity in the dorsomedial prefrontal cortex was associated with lower self-reported difficulties in emotion regulation and more partner-rated support provided in everyday life. Taken together, these findings suggest that salient self-focused information may dampen individuals’ emotional and neural responses to their partners’ uplifting and critical feedback, that emotion regulation tendencies may be an important between-subjects moderator of this effect, and that individual differences in spontaneous neural responses during this task were associated with meaningful real-world outcomes, although further validation and replication are warranted.

r7y56.authors: Binghui Li, Justina Tan Sue Ching, Kean J. Hsu
r7y56.description: Background: Post-event processing, a form of repetitive negative thinking that serves as a key maintenance factor in social anxiety, has been suggested to be connected with attentional bias to threat and negative self-evaluation. However, prior work has relied on stimuli and tasks that limit ecological validity and generalizability to real-world social experiences. This study examined how attentional bias, negative self-evaluation, and 24-hour post-event rumination are associated when socially anxious individuals undergo a brief dyadic social interaction. Method: A total of 166 participants (83 dyads, final N = 161; 81 participants with Social Anxiety Disorder and 80 healthy control participants) completed a dyadic interaction, subsequent interaction recording review, and a performance evaluation task. Participants rated self- and partner-interaction performance while gaze behavior was tracked. Results: Consistent with hypotheses, socially anxious participants reported higher state anxiety before and after the interaction and elevated post-event rumination relative to controls. Eye-tracking results revealed a significant attentional bias toward negative self-evaluation among socially anxious participants relative to controls, suggesting a self-focused attentional style that may amplify fear of negative evaluation. Both groups showed an attentional preference toward positive evaluation anchors as well. Associational variable analyses indicated that attentional bias at least partially accounted for the association between post-interaction state anxiety and post-event rumination. Discussion: These findings suggest that the cognitive mechanisms underlying state anxiety and post-event rumination in social anxiety extend beyond attentional bias. Findings suggest evaluative attention and distorted self-appraisals as key factors in the maintenance of social anxiety.

tkax5.authors: Diego Brandenberger, Paulina Zybinska, Jonas Wiesendanger, Roxane Laetitia Frund, Birgit Kleim, Bigna Lenggenhager, Marte Roel Lesur
tkax5.description: Self-compassion, i.e., relating to oneself with warmth and kindness, is often facilitated by interventions involving externalization of the self, such as mental imagery, chairwork, or more recently also immersive virtual reality (VR). The mechanisms underlying these effects, however, remain unclear and appear theoretically paradoxical, as they may involve both self-distancing from and self-attribution with an externalized self-representation. Using artificial intelligence-generated deepfakes and VR, we examined how self-recognition of and self-identification with a virtual child during comforting  influence self-compassion in two pre-registered within-subject studies. Participants verbally comforted an emotionally responsive virtual child in VR. In Study 1 (N = 24), self-recognition was manipulated by depicting either participants’ own childhood face or an unfamiliar child. In Study 2 (N = 20), self-identification was manipulated through synchronous versus asynchronous visuotactile stimulation. Self-compassion and self-criticism questionnaires were accompanied by heart rate variability (HRV) and acoustic vocal features as physiological proxies of compassionate responding. Across both studies, self-compassion increased and self-criticism decreased from pre- to post-intervention, suggesting that immersive self-externalization may enhance compassionate self-relating across all experimental conditions. In Study 1, this was additionally reflected in increased HRV (RMSSD). Although neither manipulation produced robust differences in self-compassion outcomes, self-recognition was associated with greater physical and emotional closeness toward the child, increased self-connectedness, less distinct body boundaries, and heightened negative affect. Together, these findings suggest that self-externalization can reliably enhance self-compassion regardless of the degree of self-identification or self-recognition, while self-recognition may more strongly modulate affective and bod

d82gb.description: Clinically significant change is a central but ambiguous concept in clinical practice and medical education. This brief “When I say...” manuscript clarifies three practical meanings of the term: recovery, response, and interpretation using additional reference points such as patient experience, clinical judgement, functioning, and follow-up course. The article aims to help learners and clinicians distinguish detectable change from change that matters for patient care.

hxfs4.authors: Julia Sophia Neschen, Anne Grünert, Andreas Neubauer, Anna Jori Lücke
hxfs4.description:
A growing body of research examines whether short-term within-person affect dynamics, such as stress reactivity, are associated with changes in health. However, this broad question can be addressed using different study designs and statistical models and it is somewhat unclear to what extent researcher degrees of freedom on choosing among these might affect the conclusions regarding the longitudinal link between stress reactivity and health. In the present work, we examined the robustness of conclusions across various data analytic decisions and study designs using data from a measurement-burst study. Data were collected in four bursts from 2023 – 2024, each of which covered 21 days (with up to four daily beeps and an end-of-day assessment) from a sample of N = 743 young adults (16-39 years old, 70 % female) in Germany. We employed 18 multilevel structural equation models representing different combinations of researchers’ degrees of freedom, including daily vs. momentary stress reactivity, stationarity of effects, and different ways of considering mean levels of negative affect. Due to limited ethnic and socioeconomic diversity, generalization to other populations is limited. The findings suggest reciprocal associations between stress reactivity and self-reported health over several months and illustrate the incremental predictive validity of stress reactivity beyond mean levels of negative affect. The pattern of results differed when reciprocal links were assumed to be stable vs. allowed to vary over time. The study highlights the importance of carefully considering study designs and analytical approaches when investigating affect dynamics and changes in long-term developments across multiple timescales.

Keywords: Stress reactivity; health; ambulatory assessment; experience sampling; measurement burst

ef2zr.description:
This UK-based qualitative study explored sensory processing sensitivity (SPS), decision-making and self-regulation in highly sensitive persons (HSP) using the revised HSP Scale (Pluess et al., 2024) and semi-structured interviews. Data were analysed using descriptive statistics and reflexive thematic analysis (Braun & Clarke, 2022).

30 valid HSP Scale responses were obtained. Scores were broadly continuous (M = 5.24, SD = 0.98). 20 participants were classified as HSP due to the recruitment strategy, alongside six medium-sensitive and four low-sensitive; no clear boundary separated the groups. Two HSP types emerged within the HSP group (M = 5.84, SD = 0.42): Type One comprised the majority, with one to two sensitivity dimensions, whereas Type Two had three to four. Females were the majority in both types, while Type Two consisted of fewer participants and exhibited higher, broader, and more balanced mean scores. 

16 interviews were conducted, generating three themes: (a) Negative Experiences and SPS Changeability, (b) Factors act as both facilitators and barriers, and (c) Binary Tendency in Self-Regulation and Decision Control. Seven factors were identified as influencing HSP decision-making: "negative experiences", "surrounding people", "depth of processing", "emotional states", "physical overstimulation", "time", and "information". The study also questioned the revised HSP Scale’s comprehensiveness due to its lack of negative-experience items and limited capacity to identify heterogeneity among HSP.

Self-regulation was primarily employed pre-and-post decision-making, with less engagement during plan execution. Despite reporting feelings of overwhelm, most participants expressed confidence in making high-quality decisions through reflective processing and specific decision-control strategies.

s46wd.authors: Donald Lynam, David Sbarra, Jennifer L Tackett, P. Priscilla Lui, Riley McDanal, Katherine Schaumberg, Aidan G.C. Wright, Yexinyu (Yolanda) Yang, Joshua Miller
s46wd.description: Clinical psychology, like other disciplines, is facing a replication/credibility crisis that undermines the evidentiary basis of our science. The Open Science Movement (OSM) offers solutions through practices such as preregistration, Registered Reports, and sharing of data, materials, and code. Clinical psychology has been slow to adopt these reforms, leaving trainees underprepared for emerging norms. This article reviews the factors that contributed to the crisis, outlines the necessary role of transparency in distinguishing rigorous from flawed research, and documents the limited uptake of open science practices in clinical journals and graduate training. We argue that transparency is an ethical as well as methodological imperative and propose a competency-based model for embedding open science principles into doctoral education. We conclude by calling on programs, journals, and accrediting bodies to make transparency a core requirement, essential for restoring credibility and advancing a cumulative, trustworthy clinical science.
